text: A1c estimates your average blood sugar over about 3 months. A higher A1c means blood sugar has been above target more often, which can increase risk of long-term complications.
Many adults with diabetes use an A1c target near 7 percent, but goals differ by age, other conditions, and medication risk. Ask your doctor what target is right for you.
LDL is often called bad cholesterol because high levels are linked to plaque buildup in blood vessels. Lowering LDL can reduce future heart and stroke risk.
HDL helps carry cholesterol away from arteries, while high triglycerides can increase cardiovascular risk. Diet, activity, and medications can improve lipid patterns.
Hemoglobin carries oxygen in red blood cells. Low hemoglobin can cause tiredness, weakness, or shortness of breath and may need evaluation for blood loss or low iron.
White blood cells help fight infection. Very high or low values can signal infection, inflammation, medication effects, or bone marrow issues.
Creatinine is a waste product filtered by the kidneys. Higher levels can suggest reduced kidney filtration, especially when trends worsen over time.
Potassium supports heart and muscle function. Very low or very high potassium can be dangerous and may require urgent recheck or treatment changes.
Bring your latest results and ask what changed, why it changed, and what action is needed now. A clear follow-up date helps turn lab information into a care plan.
